listDisplay.expandedContent:
The primordial earth mother goddess, Coatlicue embodies the duality at the heart of Aztec religion.
                Her terrifying appearance - a skirt of writhing serpents, a necklace of human hearts and hands,
                clawed feet, and two serpent heads - represents both the life-giving earth that nurtures crops and
                the devouring grave that reclaims all living things. She is creation and destruction inseparable.

name: Aztec - Coatlicue

sections.title: Description
sections.content:
The primordial earth mother goddess, Coatlicue embodies the duality at the heart of Aztec religion.
                Her terrifying appearance - a skirt of writhing serpents, a necklace of human hearts and hands,
                clawed feet, and two serpent heads - represents both the life-giving earth that nurtures crops and
                the devouring grave that reclaims all living things. She is creation and destruction inseparable.

ochpanizltiFestival.title: The Ochpaniztli Festival
ochpanizltiFestival.description: During Ochpaniztli ('Sweeping'), the Aztecs honored Toci/Teteoinan (aspects of the earth mother). A woman was sacrificed and flayed, and a priest wore her skin to represent the goddess. This ritual related to themes of agricultural renewal - the 'death' of the old season and the 'rebirth' of the new growing cycle.

formsOfWorship.description: Hearts and blood offered to nourish the earth
Priests wore the skin of sacrificed victims to embody renewal
Ritual sweeping honored her connection to the Huitzilopochtli myth
Serpent imagery, flowers, copal incense, and first fruits

worship.relatedAspects: Her worship overlaps with related goddesses including Cihuacoatl, Tlazolteotl, and Tonantzin

cosmology.duality: Coatlicue embodies the duality at the heart of Aztec religion - life and death, creation and destruction, nurturing and devouring
cosmology.aspects: Life-giver and life-taker, nurturer and devourer - embodying cosmic duality
cosmology.role: Primordial mother goddess, mother of Huitzilopochtli, Coyolxauhqui, and the 400 southern stars

iconography.serpentSkirt: Her most defining feature - a skirt made of intertwined serpents, representing the earth's fertility and dangerous power
iconography.dualSerpentHead: Two serpent heads facing each other form her 'head' - representing blood streams from decapitation, duality, and renewal
iconography.heartNecklace: A necklace of human hearts and severed hands with a skull pendant - the earth goddess adorned with the remains of the dead
iconography.saggingBreasts: Signs of the nurturing mother who has fed many children - humanity and the gods alike
iconography.clawedHands: Eagle or jaguar claws showing her predatory, consuming aspect - the earth that devours corpses

theGreatStatue.description: The monumental stone sculpture of Coatlicue, standing nearly 9 feet tall and weighing 12 tons, is one of the most famous works of pre-Columbian art.
theGreatStatue.discovery: Discovered buried beneath Mexico City's main plaza in 1790, it was considered so terrifying that it was immediately reburied and only permanently displayed in 1823.
theGreatStatue.features: The statue depicts Coatlicue in all her terrible glory: her head is two facing serpents (representing blood flowing from her decapitated neck), she wears a necklace of human hands and hearts with a skull pendant, her skirt is woven of snakes, and her hands and feet end in claws. Yet she also displays sagging breasts - the marks of a mother who has nursed many children.
theGreatStatue.significance: This synthesis of the nurturing and the horrific makes the statue one of the most powerful religious images in world art.

extendedContent.title: Origins and Miraculous Births
extendedContent.content:
Coatlicue's origins are deeply rooted in the primordial chaos from which the Aztec cosmos emerged. She is considered the mother of the stars, the moon (Coyolxauhqui), and the sun (Huitzilopochtli). The most famous myth surrounding Coatlicue involves the miraculous birth of Huitzilopochtli. While sweeping the temple of Coatepec, she was impregnated by a ball of feathers that fell from the sky. This immaculate conception angered her other children, led by Coyolxauhqui, who saw it as a disgrace and plotted to kill her. Their mother's pregnancy was considered an affront to their honor and lineage. This narrative, detailed in the Florentine Codex, highlights the tension between the old order (represented by Coyolxauhqui and the stars) and the new order (embodied by Huitzilopochtli, the sun and patron deity of the Mexica). 

The story of Coatlicue's impregnation underscores the Aztec understanding of cyclical time and the constant struggle between opposing forces. The ball of feathers, a symbol of divinity and celestial power, represents the divine intervention that births the new age. Coatlicue's passive acceptance of this event emphasizes her role as a vessel for creation, a fertile ground for the emergence of new life. Her role is not an active one of seeking out the experience, but more so one of acceptance of the divine will. 

Furthermore, the birth narrative solidifies Huitzilopochtli's divine mandate to rule. Emerging fully armed from his mother's womb, he immediately defeats Coyolxauhqui and her siblings, scattering their bodies across the sky, thus establishing the dominance of the sun over the moon and stars. The repetition of this victory each day, as the sun rises and vanquishes the darkness, is a constant reminder of Huitzilopochtli's power and Coatlicue's role as his mother, a pivotal figure in the Aztec cosmological drama.

extendedContent.title: Iconography and Representation
extendedContent.content:
Coatlicue's iconography is among the most striking and complex in Aztec art. Her most recognizable depiction is a statue featuring a skirt of writhing serpents (hence her name, which means 'Serpent Skirt'). Her head is often replaced by two serpent heads facing each other, symbolizing the duality of life and death, creation and destruction. A necklace of human hearts and hands adorns her neck, representing the sacrifices necessary to sustain the cosmos. Her feet are depicted as claws, signifying her connection to the earth and her predatory nature as a deity who consumes life. 

The serpent skirt represents the earth's fertility and the cycle of regeneration. Serpents were associated with water, fertility, and the underworld, all crucial elements for agricultural success and the continuation of life. The necklace of human hearts and hands serves as a constant reminder of the sacrificial duties required to maintain cosmic balance. These sacrifices were not seen as acts of cruelty but as necessary offerings to nourish the gods and ensure the continuation of the world. 

Beyond the individual elements, the overall effect of Coatlicue's imagery is one of overwhelming power and terrifying beauty. She embodies the contradictions inherent in the Aztec worldview, representing both the life-giving and the life-taking aspects of the natural world. This duality is crucial to understanding her significance as a primordial earth mother goddess. The statue demands respect and reverence, inspiring both awe and fear in those who beheld it. Her depictions often emphasized the grotesque and the beautiful. Her representation is not meant to be comforting, but rather a raw embodiment of both the life-creating and life-destroying powers of the universe.

extendedContent.title: Worship and Temples
extendedContent.content:
While there were no dedicated temples solely devoted to Coatlicue, she was venerated in conjunction with other earth and fertility deities within larger temple complexes. Offerings and rituals were performed to honor her role as the mother of the gods and the provider of sustenance. Evidence suggests that she was particularly important in agricultural ceremonies, where her blessings were sought for bountiful harvests. The Templo Mayor in Tenochtitlan, the main religious structure of the Aztec capital, housed numerous shrines and altars dedicated to various deities, including those associated with fertility and agriculture, where Coatlicue would likely have been honored.

Sacrifices, both animal and human, were integral to Aztec religious practices, and Coatlicue, as a powerful earth goddess, would have received her share of these offerings. The hearts and blood of sacrificial victims were believed to nourish the gods and maintain cosmic balance. These sacrifices were often performed at the base of temple pyramids, ensuring that the earth, personified by Coatlicue, received the life force being offered. The belief was that by offering sacrifices, the Aztec people were repaying a debt to the gods for the gift of life and sustenance.

Furthermore, festivals celebrating the earth and fertility would have included rituals honoring Coatlicue. These festivals often involved music, dance, and feasting, creating a communal experience that reinforced the importance of the earth and its bounty. Priests played a crucial role in conducting these rituals and ensuring that the proper protocols were followed to appease the gods and secure their blessings. Offerings of food, flowers, and incense were also common during these festivals, demonstrating the Aztecs' gratitude for the earth's provisions.

extendedContent.title: Role in Cosmic Order
extendedContent.content:
Coatlicue’s role is fundamental to the Aztec understanding of cosmic order. She embodies the cyclical nature of existence, representing the continuous process of creation, destruction, and regeneration. As the mother of both the sun and the moon, she represents the cosmic forces that govern day and night, life and death. Her association with the earth further solidifies her role as the foundation upon which the entire cosmos rests. Without Coatlicue, the Aztec universe would be devoid of both life-giving sustenance and the necessary destruction for renewal.

The myth of Huitzilopochtli's birth, in particular, demonstrates Coatlicue's central role in maintaining cosmic balance. The defeat of Coyolxauhqui and the stars by Huitzilopochtli represents the victory of the sun over darkness, a daily occurrence that ensures the continuation of life. Coatlicue's role as the mother of Huitzilopochtli makes her an integral part of this cosmic drama, highlighting her importance in the ongoing struggle between opposing forces. She provides the divine warrior who brings the day to exist and conquer the night.

In essence, Coatlicue is the embodiment of the Aztec worldview. She represents the interconnectedness of all things, the constant cycle of change, and the necessity of sacrifice to maintain cosmic harmony. Her terrifying yet awe-inspiring appearance reflects the complex and often contradictory nature of the Aztec universe. By understanding Coatlicue, one can gain a deeper appreciation for the sophisticated and nuanced understanding of the cosmos that characterized Aztec civilization. She is a visual representation of the complex and cyclical nature of existence, encompassing creation, destruction, and rebirth in a continuous flow.

birthOfHuitzilopochtli.title: The Birth of Huitzilopochtli
birthOfHuitzilopochtli.narrative: The most famous myth of Coatlicue describes how she became pregnant while sweeping at Coatepec Hill ('Serpent Mountain'). A ball of hummingbird feathers fell from the sky, and she placed them in her bosom. When she discovered she was pregnant, her four hundred sons (the Centzon Huitznahua, representing the stars of the southern sky) and her daughter Coyolxauhqui (the moon) were outraged. Believing their mother had dishonored them, Coyolxauhqui rallied her brothers to kill Coatlicue. As they approached for the murder, Huitzilopochtli burst from his mother's womb fully armed with the fire serpent Xiuhcoatl. He slew his brothers and decapitated Coyolxauhqui, hurling her dismembered body down the slopes of Coatepec.
birthOfHuitzilopochtli.significance: Origin myth of Huitzilopochtli and the cosmic battle between sun, moon, and stars

earthAsMotherAndGrave.title: Earth as Mother and Grave
earthAsMotherAndGrave.narrative: In Aztec cosmology, the earth itself was conceptualized as Coatlicue's body - a vast being that both gives life (through crops and plants) and consumes the dead (returning them to her womb/grave). This is why her iconography combines symbols of fertility (serpents, female form) with symbols of death (skulls, severed hands, hearts). The earth that grows corn is the same earth that swallows corpses.
earthAsMotherAndGrave.significance: Human sacrifice can be understood partly as 'feeding' the earth mother, returning the blood and life that she gives to humanity through agriculture. The severed hearts offered to the gods were also offerings to the insatiable earth.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Goddess of Magic & Motherhood | Egyptian Mythology
Goddess of the Sky | Egyptian Mythology
Goddess of Love & Joy | Egyptian Mythology

worshipPractices.name: Offerings of Sustenance
worshipPractices.description: Coatlicue, as the earth mother, was offered food and other sustenance to ensure the continued fertility of the land. These offerings were meant to nourish the earth and maintain the cyclical nature of life and death. Blood sacrifices were also performed, reflecting the belief that life emerges from death.

worshipPractices.name: Ritual Cleansing
worshipPractices.description: Ritual cleansing and purification practices were likely associated with Coatlicue, given her connection to the earth's regenerative power. These rituals aimed to purify individuals and spaces, preparing them for interaction with the divine and promoting balance within the cosmos. Water and incense were common elements in these ceremonies.

festivals.name: Toxcatl
festivals.description: While primarily associated with Tezcatlipoca, Toxcatl also involved honoring the earth and its bounty, indirectly connecting to Coatlicue's role as the earth mother. The festival included dances, feasts, and the sacrifice of a young man who had been impersonating Tezcatlipoca for a year.

festivals.name: Quecholli
festivals.description: This festival, dedicated to hunting and warfare, included ceremonies honoring the earth's provision of resources. Garments associated with deities like Coatlicue were crafted and displayed, acknowledging her role in the cyclical renewal of nature and the provision of sustenance for the Aztec people.

cultural.modernLegacy: Coatlicue's image, particularly her statue, remains a powerful symbol of Aztec culture and artistic expression. She is often invoked in contemporary art and literature to represent themes of creation, destruction, duality, and the enduring strength of indigenous traditions. Her complex iconography continues to fascinate and inspire interpretations of Mesoamerican cosmology.

associations.name: Serpent
associations.significance: Serpents form her skirt, symbolizing fertility and the earth's power.

associations.name: Duality
associations.significance: She embodies the duality of life and death, creation and destruction.

associations.name: Earth
associations.significance: As the earth mother, she represents the physical world and its bounty.

associations.name: Green
associations.significance: Represents the life and fertility of the earth, linked to her role as a mother goddess.

associations.name: Sacrifice
associations.significance: Her necklace of human hearts and hands represents the necessary sacrifices for cosmic balance.

associations.name: Moon
associations.significance: She is sometimes associated with the moon, reflecting her feminine and maternal qualities.

associations.name: One
associations.significance: Represents her role as the singular, primordial mother from which all things originate.

companions.name: Huitzilopochtli
companions.description: Huitzilopochtli is Coatlicue's son and the Aztec god of war, sun, and sacrifice. He sprang forth fully armed from her womb to defeat his siblings who plotted to kill her.

companions.name: Coyolxauhqui
companions.description: Coyolxauhqui is Coatlicue's daughter and the goddess of the moon. She led her brothers, the Centzon Huitznahua, in an attempt to kill Coatlicue, but was defeated by Huitzilopochtli.

companions.name: Centzon Huitznahua
companions.description: The Centzon Huitznahua are Coatlicue's sons and the gods of the southern stars. They attempted to kill their mother, Coatlicue, along with their sister Coyolxauhqui, due to her miraculous pregnancy.

companions.name: Quetzalcoatl
companions.description: Quetzalcoatl is another of Coatlicue's sons, a major deity associated with wind, knowledge, and the morning star. He is often seen as a counterpart to his brother Huitzilopochtli.

companions.name: Chalchiuhtlicue
companions.description: Chalchiuhtlicue, the goddess of fresh water, rivers, and lakes, is often associated with Coatlicue as both are earth goddesses. They represent different aspects of the earth's power.
